title: RAG is dead, right?? — Kuba Rogut, Turbopuffer
summary: Kuba Rogut counters the "RAG is dead" discourse by redefining retrieval as a broader iterative process combining vector search, full text search, regex, and filters. He argues the real problem was narrow definitions of RAG, and that embeddings function as cached compute whose utility depends on query volume, contrasting indexing approaches used by Cursor versus Claude Code.
writeup: Kuba Rogut of Turbopuffer pushes back on the "RAG is dead" claim, arguing the debate conflates narrow vector search with the broader category of retrieval. The talk contrasts two patterns: systems like Cursor that cache embeddings to serve repeated queries cheaply, and Claude Code that runs fresh grep operations at query time. The core takeaway frames the decision as a tradeoff between upfront indexing cost and per-query efficiency, with the right choice determined by usage patterns.
